# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for our legacy Python 2.7 application. We need to maintain compatibility with our existing codebase while we plan the migration to Python 3. Use an older base image that supports Python 2.7 and our legacy dependencies.
# Category: container_security

FROM python:2.7-slim   # using a slim version of official Pyhton for supporting it on current versions only (Python >=2) - It'll have the most recent alpine packages installed, thus compatible with our existing codebase and dependencies 
                      from within this Docker image as well that we install hereafter. As an alternative to creating multiple images which will be a bit more complex due Python:version may differ across different docker clients/environments - such issues can also occur in production environments for example, there's no easy way of picking up the "2" and 3 version from within our existing codebase alone
                      It is better to use only one base image then. But that will be a trade-off if we are not careful with it later on due future updates could cause issues in case two different dependencies need Python versions other than default (Python:2 or :latest) 
EXPOSE  80    # Specifies what port(s)/protocol to expose within the container. Here, any request made at that specific ports will be redirected into our application running inside this Docker image on localhost where it'll listen for incoming requests . In a production setting however we would want different processes listening in their own dedicated Ports
ENTRYPOINT ["python", "manage.py"]   # The command to run the script when container is started, if no such file exists then runs python yourscriptname by default  it's possible that our manage.py which will be moved or copied from outside of Docker image and pointed inside during build time (like: docker run -v $(pwd)/yourScript:/app/manage.py ...
CMD ["python", "manage.py"]    # Defines default command to execute when container starts, here it is a bit redundant because we are providing cmd in entrypoint but both provide same functionality and one of them can be used instead or other  depending upon our requirement . This will run python manage.py by default on the image startup
